videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here in Vancouver, one of Canada's most beautiful cities, and in this video we're going to show you our top things to do here. So hope you enjoy it! Vancouver is located on the west coast of British Columbia in Canada. Nestled between the Pacific Ocean and the Coastal Mountains, this city has stunning natural surroundings and a vibrant urban scene. But Vancouver is also renowned for its diverse culture and multicultural neighborhoods offering a rich tapestry of cuisines, festivals, and arts. So whether you seek adventure, relaxation, or cultural immersion, this city has it all. In this video, we highlight 12 awesome things to do in and around Vancouver. Make sure to watch until the end because we also share some day trip options as well as travel tips. Now let's dive in! We are Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. Located to the northeast of downtown Vancouver, Gastown is the oldest neighborhood in the city. The area is known for its iconic steam clock, cobblestone streets, Victorian-era buildings, and charming atmosphere. The district is also home to a vibrant food and drink scene, offering a wide variety of restaurants and bars that cater to every taste, including many delicious international cuisines. However, don't limit yourself to just Gastown. Vancouver has numerous other great culinary zones, such as Yaletown and Commercial Drive. We have a blog post and Google Map with a number of great places to eat and drink. You can find a link to the blog in the description below the video. Situated in North Vancouver, Grouse Mountain provides a serene getaway from the bustling city below. You can get to the mountain by either taking the gondola or hiking the challenging Grouse Grind Trail, which is a short but steep trail with an elevation gain of 800 meters or 2,624 feet. We decided to take the gondola, which was a scenic and relaxing ride. Once you reach the top, there's a range of activities for all ages and interests, such as viewpoints, zip lines, demonstrations, the peak chairlift ride, and the grizzly bear habitat. There are currently two grizzly bears here at the top. And if you're lucky, you can actually see them from the second chairlift that goes up to the peak, because their habitat is right underneath it. Located to the south of downtown Vancouver, Kitsilano Beach is a favorite among locals and tourists alike. This sandy beach stretches for miles along English Bay, boasting a stunning view of the downtown skyline. Adjacent to the beach is Vanier Park, a spacious waterfront park known for its lush greenery and beautiful views. The park is also home to the fascinating Maritime Museum. Located on the highest point of the city, Queen Elizabeth Park offers panoramic views of Vancouver. Spanning 130 acres, this urban oasis is a great escape from the the hustle and bustle. The park features manicured gardens, vibrant flower beds, and rolling lawns, making it a perfect destination for a leisurely stroll. There's also a conservatory with many exotic plants and birds. For breathtaking views of downtown Vancouver, head to the Vancouver Lookout tower or Cypress Mountain. Both locations provide panoramic vistas that will leave you mesmerized. Cypress Mountain is a 30-minute drive from the city center and the viewpoint is located on the second switchback of Cypress Bowl Road in West Vancouver. It's one of the best and most accessible places in Vancouver for panoramic views, and above all, it's free. Vancouver is a great place for whale watching. This is because the area's diverse marine ecosystem attracts a wide variety of whale species. From the comfort of a boat, it's possible to witness humpback whales, sea lions, orcas, and many other animals in their natural habitat. Most whale watching tours depart from Granville Island and last about 5 hours. We opted for a larger boat with both outdoor and indoor seating to keep us warm. There was a limited number of passengers on board making for a more relaxed experience and also making it easier to understand all of the naturalists on the ship. We left a link to the tour we booked in the description below the video. The captain just spotted a couple of humpback whales in the distance here, so we're going to check them out now. The Lynn Canyon Suspension Bridge is situated in North Vancouver, which is a short drive away from the downtown area. The main attraction is of course the Lynn Canyon Suspension Bridge itself, which Towers 50 meters or 164 feet above the water. But there are also beautiful waterfalls, scenic viewpoints, and popular swimming holes. The best way to explore this area is by hiking the short loop trail. Although you do have to pay a small fee for parking, the park itself has no admission fee, making it an affordable alternative to the nearby Capilano Suspension Bridge, which is larger but more commercialized. Downtown Vancouver is the heartbeat of the city, boasting an array of attractions and entertainment options. You can explore the renowned Vancouver Art Gallery, go shopping along Robson Street, or sample a variety of cuisines. But if you want to delve deeper into the history and culture of Vancouver, we recommend going on a free walking tour. We joined a 3-hour guided tour, which allowed us to see the highlights of the city, such as the Waterfront Station, Robson Square, and the Vancouver Public Library. We also learned about the indigenous heritage, gaining a deeper appreciation for the region's diverse culture. Find a link to the tour we joined in the description below the video. And if you have time during your lunch break of the tour, we highly recommend trying a famous Japadog on Robson Street. Situated beneath the Granville Street Bridge, Granville Island is a formal industrial area that is transformed into a thriving cultural hub. One of it its highlights is the Public Market, a haven for food lovers. Here, you can find fresh produce, gourmet foods, local treats, and a variety of international cuisine. Apart from the market, this neighborhood is also home to many art galleries, souvenir shops, restaurants, and Grandville Island Brewing. Nestled along the shores of the Indian Arm in North Vancouver, Deep Cove is a quaint waterfront village. It's home to a small cove, a beach, charming shops, cafés, and restaurants. But above all, it's the starting point of a popular trail: the Quarry Rock hike. This 4 kilometers or 2.5 miles out-and-back trail winds through a lush forest to a stunning viewpoint overlooking the Indian Arm. It takes about 1.5 hours to do the hike, so there's plenty of time to check out the town after. Situated at the northwestern edge of the downtown area, Stanley Park is Vancouver's first, largest, and most beautiful urban park. Boasting beaches, evergreen forests, lush gardens, and cultural landmarks, Stanley Park provides a perfect blend of natural beauty and recreational opportunities. The best way to discover the park is by biking the scenic Seawall, spanning 9 kilometers or 5.5 miles. You can rent bikes at one of the many shops near the entrance of Stanley Park. We rented ours for 3 hours, which was more than enough time to make stops in the park and soak in the surroundings. Finally, don't miss the opportunity to embark on a day trip to Vancouver's nearby Provincial Parks or mountain towns. There's so much to explore outside the city, from the Shannon Falls and the Sea-to-Sky Gondola in Squamish, to the stunning landscapes of Whistler, Lions Bay, and Golden Ears. Our favorite hiking areas are the Garibaldi and Joffre Lakes Provincial Parks. These excursions will leave you in awe of BC's natural beauty. If you don't have access to a car, it is possible to join one of the many guided day tours from Vancouver. As we promised at the beginning of the video, we'll now share a few travel tips for Vancouver. Vancouver is generally a safe city, but there are some neighborhoods and streets that you should avoid because of high crime rate and homelessness. This includes Downtown Eastside, which borders Gastown. We left a link to an article that highlights all of those areas in the description below the video. Second, Vancouver has an efficient public transportation system which includes buses and the SkyTrain. They're easy to use as you can just tap your credit card when you get on. We recommend using Google Maps to navigate the public transit routes effectively. Finally, Vancouver is an expensive city, especially considering accommodation, food, drinks, and activities. So take advantage of the city's public transportation system to save costs. Also consider staying in a hostel or a place outside the city center, close to one of the major SkyTrain lines like the Canada Line. That way, you still have easy access to the city center. We included some great budget options in the description below the video. If you're planning your Vancouver trip, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code in the description below. We also included other important links in the description that will help you with your trip, such as an interactive map to get to each place that we mentioned. We'll catch you on the next adventure!

controlled environment this is really a great place for the entire family as well as a great place to escape the cold chilly days and for us we especially loved all the beautiful birds many of which reminded us of some of the Exotic destinations that we visited over the years are not far from the Queen Elizabeth Park another top attraction that we've never visited created in 1966 by a group of gardening enthusiasts the Van Dusen botanical gardens inspires understanding of the vital importance of plants to all life through their Botanical collections programs and practices we really enjoyed walking around the gardens and taking a break from the hustle and bustle of the city it's such a peaceful place and I can see why it has so many great reviews well the Van Dusen Gardens were really beautiful but it's unfortunate that we were here kind of an early spring obviously a lot of the flowers have not started to bloom yet but I can really imagine that that place must look much more spectacular in late spring summer and probably in the fall as well right now we're actually at Sunset Beach a really popular Beach that's pretty much in the heart of Vancouver that's another one of the things we highly recommend to do while you're here is to go check out one of the beaches you'll find some in the kitchen Beach is really popular there's beaches over in Stanley Park but I find Sunset Beach is the one that's like full of life like there's people playing hockey across from me right now there's people playing beach volleyball some people have their dogs out or just hanging out on the beach and this is kind of one of the cool things that I always find about Vancouver because in Calgary we don't have this so just being able to relax on a beach is a pretty cool way to spend the day and to watch sunset this is actually our last night in Vancouver on this trip but of course we've been here many other times so a few other things we would recommend of course is visiting Gastown that's one of the most popular neighborhoods in the city and it's basically where Vancouver got its start that's why it's a National Historic Site you'll actually find like cobblestone streets really old brick buildings and it's just a beautiful area to walk around with lots of shops and restaurants and of course the famous steam clock which is one of very few in the world [Music] another place I'd highly recommend visiting is the Museum of anthropology at the University of British Columbia we've been there before right now it's actually closed until late 2023 for renovations but it's such a great place to see both traditional and contemporary art from the northwest coast First Nations as well as different cultures from around the world when we were there I think the thing that really stood out to us the most was all the totem poles and last but not least there's another popular attraction called the Capilano suspension bridge that's not far from downtown Vancouver it's really popular because it's about 140 meters long and it's suspended about 70 meters over the Capilano River people seem to love walking across that and they even have other activities there as well such as a cliff walk and a treetop Adventure but yeah other than that of course we've just listed some of the best things to do in the city especially that are kind of easy to do especially if it's videos.transcript: known for its friendly locals and Scenic views Vancouver is a gorgeous City to explore this is Katie from creative travel guide and today we share the things to know before you go to Vancouver [Music] looking for more travel tips make sure to hit subscribe and ring that notification Bell so firstly let's look at the best time to visit June to September is the best time to visit Vancouver the weather is warm and there is only a small chance of rain you can expect around 13 hours of warm sunshine perfect for days exploring the outdoor sites however this is the most popular time for visitors so expect crowds march to May is a good time to visit if you are looking for a quieter experience spring brings with it Pleasant weather and beautiful flowers out in the Parks hotel rates drop during October to November so visit at this time if you are on a budget whilst December to February is ideal for those looking to enjoy the winter sports so now let's look at getting there most visitors arrive in Vancouver via air landing at the Vancouver International Airport which is located just 25 minutes from downtown you can find plenty of international flights into the city especially from the US from the airport you could choose to take a taxi from the taxi stand on level 2 or take the SkyTrain Canada Line into downtown Vancouver and parts of Richmond Canadian road trips are something very special indeed rent a car or RV and drive to Vancouver using the trans-canada highway you could also choose to take the train to Vancouver with the rocky Mountaineer being a popular option for those wanting to see the best sights in British Columbia amtraks are also available from Portland and Seattle in the US so now let's look at getting around Vancouver is easy to navigate meaning you can walk around the downtown area with ease and rent the city bikes to travel a little faster if you are choosing to cycle around the city just remember to not ride on the sidewalk and always wear a helmet you could also choose to use the public transportation options the TransLink system includes buses the sky train and the Sea bus Ferry all of which are reliable and affordable get yourself a compass card if you are planning on staying in the city for a while this is a reloadable travel card that can be used for one-way fares and day passes you could also take a taxi or use Lyft or Uber for Journeys slightly further away whilst driving is a popular option for those looking to see some of the amazing national parks in British Columbia so now let's look at typical prices hostile dorm beds begin at 54 Canadian dollars in good locations whilst private rooms and hostels start around 130. mid-range three-star hotel start at around two hundred dollars what's more luxurious options begin at 280 Canadian dollars eating out can be a traveler's biggest cost in Canada a quick breakfast from Tim Hortons begins around five dollars whilst a sit-down brunch will cost at least twenty dollars per person lunch begins at twenty dollars as well whilst a sit down dinner begins at around 35 per person but can be much more than that depending on your chosen restaurant Vancouver and in fact any city in Canada can be an expensive place to explore there are ways to save money firstly stick to walking around the site the downtown area is relatively compact so you can walk between many of the major sites visit in the winter to get better priced hotels and use budget Airlines like WestJet to fly into the City without spending too much you could save money on accommodation by booking a mid-range hotel room outside of the city but along the sky train route then use the sky train to get in and out of the downtown area opt to eat at the Asian restaurants for cheaper meals and pay attention to the happy hour deals in the bars get yourself a Vancouver 24 or 48 hour Hop On Hop Off sightseeing bus to see the sights and get around the city with ease what's the Vancouver attraction passport can save you up to 50 in over 50 attractions and restaurants we will leave both links in the description below so now let's look at the best things to do Vancouver is home to have plenty of things to see and do firstly spend some time exploring Stanley Park that includes a Botanical Garden an aquarium and beaches you could choose to bike along the exterior sea wall too head over to Granville Island for a fantastic Public Market Restaurants artist Studios and Galleries and then from May to October you could choose to visit the Richmond Night Market known for its amazing food the Chinese Garden on the Edge of Chinatown is a beautiful place to visit in the city whilst whale watching is a must here as well catch a hockey game at Rogers Arena see the city from above on Grouse Mountain and then explore at least one of the museums in the city head to the suspension bridges in Lynn Canyon and take a walk through the Pacific Spirit Regional Park as well watch this video to find out how to plan a trip to Canada then to learn about the best places to visit in Canada watch this video we hope you have an incredible time in Vancouver and stay creative

videos.transcript: Hello friends, welcome back to another video. We are here in Vancouver and if you have 2 days to spend in this beautiful city, here's exactly how to do it. [Music] We start day one by heading to the English Bay neighborhood and a visit to Cafe Portrait. This is a super cozy family-owned cafe that's as much art gallery as it is a breakfast spot. And all the portraits on the walls are painted by the owner's father. But we came specifically to try their traditional Turkish coffee brewed over hot sand. It was delicious. Our breakfast was great, too. Chris had the shakshuka and I enjoyed Turkish eggs with sausage and that warm garlic yogurt. Great spot. Fueled up, we traded caffeine for cardio and rented bikes using Vancouver's Moby Bike Share program. It's quick and easy. You just download the app, pick up your bike at any of the docking stations around town, and ride away. We did this because for about 2 hours, we cruised along the famous Vancouver Seaw Wall. And I think no visit to Vancouver is complete without doing this. The seaw wall circles Stanley Park, which is a 1,000 acre park with beaches and towering fur and cedar forests, indigenous landmarks, and sweeping views of the city, mountains, and ocean. But if you don't want to bike it, you can also hike or walk the trails throughout the park as well. We ended our bike ride kind of back where we started at English Bay at the Sylvia Hotel, a relic from the early 1900s with a great patio that looks out over Sunset Beach. It was the perfect place to relax with a drink and take in the views. And for a bit of a late lunch, we found a great spot called Lucky Sushi Co, which we had spotted earlier that day because it was just down the street from Cafe Portrait. It's a retro inpired isizakaya and casual spot for sushi and it has a stunning view of English bay. So try to snag a window spot. And the menu had lots of unique items for something different. Did it work? Oh no, it's professional. >> It was going to be a beautiful evening. So, after biking around town, we decided to upgrade our ride to a 50-foot yacht. First, we took a little water taxi to head to the departure point for our evening sunset cruise. I found this through Get Your Guide, and as soon as I saw that it was adults only and a group size of up to only nine people and a drink included, I had to book it. We sailed past Granville Island, out through English Bay, and along the edges of Stanley Park. We had the skyline on one side, the mountains on the other. It was the perfect way to enjoy the sunset. It kind of felt like we accidentally joined someone's rich uncle's boat trip. It was super cool. So, if you want to enjoy a chill evening, I'll leave a link to the tour that we booked. We're here at Rodney's Oyster House, which is just around the corner from the Opus Hotel. and we're here to try the best of British Columbia, which is West Coast oysters and some wine. If you're ever in Vancouver or British Columbia, you need to try the wine because very little of it gets exported. So, if you like a good sav blanc or pino green, you need to try it. This place is a must visit for seafood lovers. Our home base for this trip was the Opus Hotel in the Yale Town neighborhood. A boutique spot that had pretty good reviews, and after three nights here, we would absolutely stay again. Purple. We were welcomed with a glass of sparkling wine at check-in, and that was just the start of how great we thought this hotel was. Our room was compact, but it was thoughtful. The bed was cloud level cozy. I loved the bold splash of purple, and it had a great bathroom. It had this massive window, so the room felt big and airy with all the natural light coming in. There were heated floors and a rain shower head that actually had pressure. And the location couldn't be better. It was across the street from the subway station, which gets you to the airport in 20 minutes, and you're surrounded by dozens of great restaurants and cafes. One thing about hotels in Vancouver, though, is that they are expensive. So, I recommend booking as early as you can and shopping around for the best deals. I like using booking or plan in for the best price, which is how we found this hotel. If you want to book this spot, I'll leave links below or you can scan the QR code. To start day two, many travel guides will tell you to visit the Capalano Suspension Bridge, one of Vancouver's most famous attractions. It's 230 ft above the river, swaying between rainforest treetops. It's very cool, no question, and I do think it's worth doing once. But I don't love heights or crowds, so we gave it a pass this time. If you're fine with both, though, go early for the best experience. Or better yet, check out the suspension bridge at Lin Canyon instead. No trip to Vancouver is complete without a visit to Granville Island. Of course, the best way to get there is on one of the fun water taxis. >> You have to wheel. I love these. >> So much fun. >> Here at Granville Island, it's part artist colony, part market, part foodie Disneyland. Inside, you'll find stalls overflowing with local crafts and homemade goods and jewelry and about a million things to snack on. I can never pass by local handmade soap. And we snacked on artisan pickles and maple candied salmon. We even found a nice spot to try small batch sake made from locally grown rice. Around the island there are buskers and live music, galleries, shops, restaurants, and cafes to discover. It's definitely an easy way to spend an entire afternoon. I will say it gets pretty busy and it can kind of be overwhelming because you don't want to miss all the cool spots. So, there are a couple of local tours that will show you around and kind of get you VIP access to some of the stalls and a lot of tastings. So, I'll leave a link to one of those great tours below. After grazing our way through the market, it was time to swap the food stalls for more of Vancouver's scenery. We actually had a sea plane tour booked, but the weather wasn't cooperating, so we'll have to do it next time. But either way, it's still fun to hang out at Cole Harbor to watch the planes take off and land along the waterfront. If you're lucky enough to get a sunny day, this would be such a cool way to see the skyline and the mountains and the ocean all in one shot. So, I'll leave a link to the tour that we had planned because it's definitely something I'd love to do on my next visit. That afternoon, we wandered into Gas Town, Vancouver's oldest neighborhood. There's brick buildings, narrow alleyways, and the famous steam clock that hisses on the corner. But we had a specific mission in mind. Casa Donatada, a tiny cafe serving Portuguese custard tarts. I've been chasing these since my visit to Portugal last year, and these were dangerously good. I would say this is going to be almost as close as you'll get without actually crossing the Atlantic. I will say Gas Town's a bit rougher around the edges these days, but the food scene makes it worth exploring. Whether you join a walking food tour or pop into a bar or a patio for a drink or just sit back and people watch. By the way, if you want to check out all the places that we visited in this video, I've got them all written down on my blog post, so you can check that itinerary. Plus, I have a few extra details and things for you there as well. So, I'll leave that linked below or you can scan the QR code. Vancouver is one of the most sushi crazed cities in the world, so if you're a fan, you will be spoiled with choices. I'll let you fight it out in the comments about where the best spot is, but we ended up at Sushi Maro just across from our hotel, and we loved it. No matter where you end up for sushi, though, try the BC roll if it's on the menu. It's made with barbecued salmon skin, cucumber, and a sweet sauce rolled up uramaki style, and it's delicious. I hope you enjoyed our mini tour of Vancouver. If you have a little more time in the area or if you're headed there next, we have a great video about visiting Victoria on Vancouver Island, which is where we also did some whale watching. So, I've got a couple great videos. I'll leave those linked below for you to watch next. And thank you so much for watching, and we'll see you in the next one.

Now, in case you're new here, hi, I'm Christina from HappyToWander.com and this channel is all about practical travel tips from someone who's been there, done that. So, be sure to like and subscribe if that sounds like your kind of thing. Now, before we start, I do want to acknowledge that the city of Vancouver is situated on the unseated traditional territories of the Musqueam, Squamish, and Tsleil-Waututh nations, so be sure to incorporate some activities involving indigenous culture and history if you can. Indigenous Tourism BC is a great resource for finding indigenous owned and run businesses, but some great starting points in Vancouver include the Bill Reid Gallery of Northwest Coast Art downtown and the Museum of Anthropology at UBC, as well as Salmon n' Bannock for First Nations Cuisine, if you want to try it. They even have a location at the airport, actually, if you want to grab it before you head out. Now, let's get started with some Vancouver travel planning tips, beginning with when to visit. Now, I'm definitely biased, but I do think of Vancouver as a great year-round destination, even though haters will say that it isn't. It honestly all depends on what you're coming for, though. Most people will recommend summer as the ideal time to visit because the weather is, of course, at its best for all the outdoorsy activities that the city is known for, and there's a lot of great events as well, like street festivals, and the annual Celebration of Light's fireworks, but honestly, spring and fall can be great times to explore, too. You just have to layer up a bit and maybe expect a higher chance of rain. Fall, of course, comes with the perk of the beautiful colors and foliage from October to November, while spring from March to April usually means gorgeous cherry blossoms all over the city. May and June, in my opinion, are also really solid months where you get good weather and fewer crowds compared to July and August and more tolerable temperatures, which is very important because Vancouver was not built for high temperatures, okay, the buses are a nightmare in the summer, and AC isn't really a standard in most houses or apartments, so heat waves can definitely be a challenge. So, yes, there's no bad time to come so long as you dress for the weather and aren't scared of random rain. The only time I might avoid if you're not into snow sports at all is winter because it can get really gloomy here, but there's still lots of fun events like the Christmas festivities, then annual events like Dine Out Vancouver, where restaurants have set menus for a cheap price, and the hot chocolate festival as well, so don't stress, Vancouver can be amazing any time of year. In terms of how long to stay, there's a ton to do in Metro Vancouver, which includes these 21 municipalities. And while you're here, you'll definitely want to get out of the city to explore more nature and scenery around, so I'd personally say 5 days, if not a week minimum. If we're talking just the main sites in and around downtown Vancouver, though, 2 days is probably enough. Next, let's talk about where to stay. Downtown Vancouver is definitely the main area of choice for most first-time visitors just because it's close to the sites. But to be honest, it is the most expensive and some parts of downtown are just frankly not that nice to stay in. If you want to stay downtown, I would recommend picking somewhere around Coal Harbor, the West End, or Yaletown. Basically, anywhere closer to the water. The central downtown core itself can be fairly chaotic and busy, and honestly, not the most comfortable to walk around after dark. Though on or around Robson Street is usually fine. All that said, if you don't mind using public transport, then any hotel that is near a SkyTrain station will get you downtown reasonably quickly and might be a better option. My personal recommendation is somewhere around Lonsdale Quay in North Vancouver. The hotels here can be way cheaper than downtown, and you're only a 12-minute sea bus ride from Waterfront Station. The commute is also stunning, plus you're close to lots of nature-oriented activities once you're on that side of the water. Now, let's move on to Vancouver transport tips and getting around. If you're flying into Vancouver, then the good news is getting to the city center from the airport couldn't be easier. The SkyTrain will get you straight downtown in about 25 minutes, and the cost is about $10, which includes a $5 charge that they apply when you get on at stops closer to the airport. All the payment terminals do accept contactless cards now, so you can just tap in and go. But that said, it might be worth getting a day pass if you plan to use public transport multiple times the day you arrive. You can also get a taxi, though do note that flat rates do apply depending on where you're going, so don't get scammed by drivers trying to charge you more. I'll link that flat rate map down in the description. Now, people in Vancouver typically prefer using ride share apps over regular taxis because the taxi companies in this city don't have the best reputation, but I have heard okay things about the new Yellow Cab App if you're looking for an alternative to ride shares. All that said, I'd advise against just hopping into a taxi on the street. Now, the public transport system in Vancouver is run by TransLink and includes over 250 bus routes and three SkyTrain lanes, plus the SeaBus over to North Vancouver. One ticket gets you on all these different modes of transport and something I love is that the tickets are time-based, meaning you can do a roundtrip journey on one ticket so long as it's within 90 minutes. As I mentioned before, Vancouver's transport system is compatible with contactless payments, so for short trips, you can just use any credit, debit, or digital wallet as well. This takes the guesswork out of things like zones and peak versus off- peak pricing because you just tap in and it'll charge you accordingly. Though, I do have to say the fares are much cheaper if you get something called a Compass Card, which is a reloadable card that you can use on public transport. You do have to pay a $6 deposit on these, but you can return them when you leave for a full refund at the customer service center at Waterfront Station, which is conveniently on your way back to the airport. The savings can add up, but honestly, it probably doesn't make sense to do this and go to this trouble unless it's a longer trip where you expect to take a lot of public transport. Again, another option is to get a DayPass for all zones all day. Usually worth it if you plan to do two or more trips in one day across two zones. Since fares are calculated automatically for you when you tap in and out, you probably don't need to worry about zones too much, but just for your information, on the SkyTrain, zones do matter, so the farther you travel, the more you pay. But buses are all one zone, you just tap in at the start and that's it, no need to tap out. Whereas on the SkyTrain, there will be fare gates both to enter and to leave, so have those cards handy. And it's worth noting as well that travel on weekends and holidays, as well as after 6:30 p.m. on weekdays, are considered one zone across the board, so that means cheaper travel. All that said, honestly, if you're staying just in Vancouver proper, seeing the main sites in and around downtown, then you'll probably be absolutely fine to get by with public transport using Uber, Lyft, or taxis to fill in any gaps in between. If, however, you do want to go out and explore some of the nature, then renting a car will give you the most flexibility. I usually use Discover Cars because they let you search and compare a bunch of companies at once to find the best deal. You can also look into private tours to bring you to more car-reliant places if you don't want to rent a car as well. Now, finally, before we move on, there are some privately owned modes of transport worth noting in Vancouver as well. There are the small ferries, for instance, that operate in False Creek. Both False Creek Ferries, which are the blue ones, and the Aquabus, which is the rainbow one. On a nice day, I love taking these, especially to get to Granville Island or Vanier Park. It's a super scenic journey, so it makes the price tag really worth it, although, of course, normal buses are going to be cheaper. Now, let's move on to Vancouver food and drink tips. If there's one thing this city is known for, it is the food. In terms of what's good to try in Vancouver, yes, there are the typical staples of West Coast cuisine, so things like fresh seafood, but an overwhelming theme to most chain restaurants, especially downtown, is something that people call 'casual fine dining,' so restaurants with a nice vibe, but menus featuring a little bit of everything, so some burgers and sandwiches, some pastas, some steaks, you know the deal. Cactus Club, Earl's, and The Keg for steak would be the best known chains for this, and they're reliable, but honestly, there are better places to eat, in my opinion, if we're talking strictly about food. One really great thing about Vancouver is how diverse the city is, along with the food culture as well, so be sure to explore that while you're here. You can find so many different cuisines in this city, but honestly, to me, I think there are few things more quintessentially Vancouver than sushi. I think everybody in the city has their go-to decent sushi place that they frequent and there are so many options, but downtown, I would say Tom Sushi is a good bet, and Miku or Minami if you wanted something a bit more on the luxurious side, especially for their signature aburi style sushi, which is lightly torched and so delicious. Other quintessentially Vancouver foods you'll find in and around downtown include Japa Dogs which are hot dogs with Japanese toppings, Caesars, which are Canada's national cocktail, and the Score on Davie is a classic place for them with so many options. It's also very close to La Belle Patat, which is my go-to spot for poutine in the city, and that's fries topped with gravy and cheese curds, another Canadian specialty. There's also the butter beef and fried chicken from Phnom Penh in Chinatown, and the donuts from Mello are also amazing if you're in this area already, as well as the ice cream from Earnest Ice Cream, which has a few different locations. There's honestly so much though beyond that, and we'd be here for hours if I just talked about all the food places I like, so try as much as possible. Ramen, pho, dim sum, Indian, you got it. You can check out the full written guide for more recommendations. And I know these tips are so specific, but if you love food as much as I do and are still with me, be sure to comment 'foodie' below. Craft beer is another big thing in Vancouver, although quality definitely varies. But if you're looking for a very local activity, brewery hopping is definitely a big thing, especially around the Mount Pleasant neighborhood, where you'll find loads in a walkable area. And the city is beyond obsessed with brunch as well. Jam Café and OEB are popular casual favorites, whereas Maxine's is good if you want something higher-end. Now, without a doubt, dining out in Vancouver can get expensive, especially because the standard tip here is about 20% these days. So, if you're looking to save some money, happy hour is definitely the way to go. These specials typically run from 2:00 to 5:00 p.m. or sometimes 3:00 to 6:00 and involves great deals on food and drink. Especially because as tourists, you have a lot more flexibility with your schedule, this can be a great way to eat and drink for way less. Some places will also do all day happy hours on non-peak days like Monday or Tuesday and other great specials throughout the week, so be sure to do some research and keep an eye out. And this is a very niche tip, but if you want a super filling and affordable bite downtown, the Costco in downtown Vancouver across from Rogers Arena is one of the few left that offers food court access for everyone, even those without a membership, so yes, we're talking $1.50 hot dog and drink combos, plus the best soft serve ice cream, $2.99 pizza slices, you name it, and obviously, it's good, so this is an especially nice option if you're going to an event at Rogers Arena or BC Place - definitely a local rite of passage, so you can kind of pass it off as a cultural experience. Next, let's talk about Vancouver tips for attractions. Not going to lie, sightseeing in Vancouver is so expensive. There's unfortunately not many ways around this if you want to see those specific paid spots like the Aquarium or Science World because, unlike other large cities, there aren't any sightseeing passes that will save you a great deal of money. But luckily, if you're flexible, some of the best things to do in the city happen to be free. So, if budget is a concern, I would personally prioritize free activities first rather than spend hundreds of dollars going to museums and paid sites. In terms of free things to do, walking or biking the Stanley Park Seawall is a must. I really don't think you get the full Stanley Park experience without doing the whole seawall, so be sure to check that out. And if you're open to extending your journey, continuing along the seawall to False Creek or starting earlier by the convention center and Coal Harbor is an incredibly scenic walk. Another gorgeous park is Queen Elizabeth Park, with great views of the skyline and the very underrated Bloedel Conservatory, which is one of the more affordable paid attractions in Vancouver. I should say, there's also free alternatives to paid sites as well. The Capilano Suspension Bridge, for instance, is beautiful and it really is a special place, but at over $70 per person, it's quite pricey, especially for families. But North Vancouver is full of gorgeous trails, and Lynn Canyon has a suspension bridge of its own that's free. Sure, it's not as long and not as epic, but the scenery there is unreal and it's free, so that's a great alternative. There's also so many great views that you don't really need to pay for, so while the Vancouver Lookout does have a nice view, you can get more impressive views elsewhere. If you have a car, the viewpoint at Cypress Mountain and the new Trestle Bridge is gorgeous. And Burnaby Mountain Park is always a winner. Downtown, there's also actually a free rooftop terrace at the main Vancouver Public Library that most people don't know about, so yes, I'll link my full list of Vancouver must-dos in the description, though do keep in mind that there are probably way better alternatives to the typical guidebook must-dos, like the Gas Town Steam Clock, which is just okay. And if you plan to do a day trip or two while you're here, I know Victoria over on Vancouver Island is usually the most popular choice, but to be honest with you, that's a super long day. An hour and a half on the ferry each way, plus time to get between the ferry terminals and the main city on both sides. Listen, I love Victoria and I think it's beautiful, but if you want to make the most of a day with minimal traveling, I'd actually recommend a day trip to either Bowen Island if you don't have a car, or the Sunshine Coast if you do. Both have ferry sailings from Horseshoe Bay, which has an express bus to and from downtown, and that takes about 35 minutes. And the journey times are considerably shorter as well. The views you get along the way happen to also be amazing, so that would be my recommendation. There are also tons of other amazing spots all around Metro Vancouver and the Fraser Valley as well, suitable for day trips too, so don't just limit yourself to Victoria. Again, you can check out my full Vancouver guide for more recommendations. In terms of outdoor attractions, because I know that's a big draw for first-time visitors, there are some hikes in Vancouver that are so popular, like the Grouse Grind, for instance, but I assure you, there are hundreds, literally hundreds of nicer hikes. The Grouse Grind is all stairs, and you don't get any nice views along the way really because it's all trees, so I wouldn't recommend this one unless you want the bragging rights or you want to bypass the super pricey gondola trip up to the top. So again, look into alternatives, especially if you want to avoid crowds in busier periods. The AllTrails app is a great resource for that. And if you have a car and plan to visit places like provincial parks, do note that some of the more popular ones do actually require you to reserve a day use pass for parking in advance. Alright, now let's move on to some Vancouver tips for when you arrive. Firstly, do make sure you carry two pieces of ID if you plan to visit bars, clubs, and buy alcohol. Most bars and clubs will ask for ID on busy evenings and sometimes even restaurants if you're going to be sitting in a lounge or bar area. And we do require two pieces of ID in BC most of the time, usually one with a photo and one without, so a driver's license and a credit card would work fine. I'd also make sure you have at least 30 bucks cash on you just in case. Most places, especially downtown, will take card, most are even card only, but if you plan on visiting any smaller restaurants or food court hidden gems, especially when it comes to Asian food, a lot of the best places will be cash only, and you don't want to be caught out without being able to pay. And as I mentioned before, tipping culture here is unfortunately out of control. Usually, the option to tip comes up on the card machine with suggested percentages, these days 18% being a very common minimum, so just keep that in mind. You can always input a custom tip of less, but a lot of the time, people won't be too happy about that. Now, in terms of navigating, do be aware that Metro Vancouver is composed of many separate cities, so if you're entering anything on a map, double check that the address chosen is for the correct city because there are so many streets using a numbered system, like let's say West 15th Street, and there will be a different West 15th Street in North Vancouver compared to Vancouver, for instance, so always double check. And another thing to take note of so you don't get confused, West Vancouver, the West End, and the West Side of Vancouver are actually all different places, just a local quirk to be mindful of. Finally, it has to be said, Vancouver is a city that, like many other big cities, has issues around unhoused individuals, mental illness, and addiction. Depending on where you're from, seeing this in person can be quite shocking because it is a fairly visible issue that you'll encounter, certainly downtown. Some areas to definitely avoid include East Hastings and the eastern end of Gastown and unfortunately Granville Street, which used to be regarded as a hub of Vancouver nightlife, but really isn't the most pleasant place to walk anymore. All that said, most of the time these individuals are not violent, so you don't need to worry too much about your personal safety, but it's still something to be mindful of. Alright, thanks so much for watching, be sure to like and subscribe for more travel videos just like this one, and I'll see you next time. Bye!
